note_data.title: Cells and Tissues
keyPoints: Cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life — all living organisms are made up of cells
Cell theory states: (1) All living things are made of cells (2) Cell is the basic unit of life (3) All cells arise from pre-existing cells
Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound nucleus and organelles — found in bacteria and blue-green algae
Eukaryotic cells have membrane-bound nucleus and organelles — found in plants, animals, fungi and protists
Cell membrane is selectively permeable — allows some substances to pass through while blocking others
Mitochondria is called powerhouse of cell — produces ATP energy through cellular respiration
Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis in plant cells
Tissue is a group of similar cells performing the same function
Meristematic tissue consists of actively dividing cells found at growing points of plants
Permanent tissues are formed from meristematic tissues and have lost the power of division

definitions.definition: Cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life. It is the smallest unit that can be called living.
A tissue is a group of similar cells that perform the same function.
Prokaryotic cells are those cells which lack membrane-bound nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles.
Eukaryotic cells are those cells which have membrane-bound nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles.
Meristematic tissue consists of actively dividing cells and is found at the growing points of plants.

importantQuestions.question: Write any three points of cell theory.
importantQuestions.answer: (1) All living things are made up of one or more cells (2) Cell is the basic unit of life (3) All cells arise from pre-existing cells by division.

importantQuestions.question: Differentiate between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells (any 5 points).
importantQuestions.answer: Prokaryotic Cells: (1) Lack membrane-bound nucleus (2) Genetic material freely distributed in cytoplasm (3) No membrane-bound organelles (4) Ribosomes are 70S type (5) Found in bacteria and blue-green algae. Eukaryotic Cells: (1) Have membrane-bound nucleus (2) Genetic material enclosed in nucleus (3) Have membrane-bound organelles (4) Ribosomes are 80S type (5) Found in plants, animals, fungi and protists.

mcqs.question: Which organelle is called the powerhouse of the cell?
The genetic material in prokaryotic cells is:

commonMistakes: Students often confuse prokaryotic with eukaryotic — remember PRO means 'before' (before nucleus formation)
Writing incomplete cell theory — all three points must be mentioned
Confusing meristematic with permanent tissue — meristematic divides, permanent doesn't
Not drawing clear labeled diagrams of plant and animal cells
Mixing up functions of organelles — mitochondria for energy, chloroplast for photosynthesis
examTips: Cell theory appears in every BSEK paper — memorize all three points exactly
Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic differentiation is a favorite 5-mark question
Always draw neat labeled diagrams — they carry 2-3 marks
Meristematic tissue location and function frequently asked in MCQs
Learn exact definitions word-by-word as given in STBB textbook
note_data.keyDiagram: Plant Cell and Animal Cell: Must show and label nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, cell wall (plant only), vacuole, mitochondria, chloroplasts (plant only), ribosomes. Draw large clear circles with proper labels and leader lines.
pastPaperRefs: BSEK 2023 SSC-II Q3 (4 marks) — asked students to define cell theory and list its three main postulates
BSEK 2022 SSC-II Q7 (6 marks) — required differentiation between plant and animal cells with labeled diagrams
BSEK 2021 SSC-II Q4 (5 marks) — asked for definitions of tissue, organ and organ system with examples
BSEK 2020 SSC-II Q2 (3 marks) — tested knowledge of prokaryotic vs eukaryotic cell differences

workedExample.question: Define tissue. Differentiate between simple and complex tissues in plants. (6 marks)
steps: Write the exact textbook definition of tissue
Create a comparison table showing differences
workedExample.answer: Tissue: A group of similar cells that perform the same function is called tissue. Simple Tissues: Tissues composed of only one type of cells are called simple tissues. Examples: Parenchyma, Collenchyma, Sclerenchyma. Complex Tissues: Tissues composed of more than one type of cells are called complex tissues. Examples: Xylem and Phloem.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for tissue definition, 2 marks for simple tissue definition and examples, 2 marks for complex tissue definition and examples, 1 mark for clear presentation

examinerWarnings: Students often lose marks by writing general answers instead of textbook-exact definitions. Always use the STBB textbook phrasing for full marks.
Many students confuse organelles with organs - remember organelles are cell structures while organs are made of different tissues working together.
When drawing cell diagrams, students frequently miss labeling key structures like cell wall, chloroplasts, or vacuoles which costs easy marks.
note_data.quickRecap: Cell is the basic unit of life existing as prokaryotic or eukaryotic types. Plant cells have cell wall, chloroplasts and large vacuole unlike animal cells. Tissues are groups of similar cells performing same function, classified as simple or complex in plants. Organs are made of different tissues while organ systems contain multiple organs working together.
